id: bigtech:the artemis ii mission has ended where does nasa go from her:2026-04-11T03:24:21.000Z
title: The Artemis II mission has ended. Where does NASA go from here?
author: Eric Berger
summary_long: The Artemis era well and truly began Friday evening when a shiny spacecraft that had traveled 700,000 miles around the Moon, carrying four astronauts, splashed down in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California. For NASA, for its international partners, and for all of humanity the successful conclusion of the Artemis II mission marked a return to deep space by our species after more than half a century. It was a spectacular achievement, and NASA deserves credit for making something what is very difficult look relatively easy. But it also raises an important question: What comes next? Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-04-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: bigtech:proiran explosive media trolls trump with aigenerated lego c:2026-04-10T13:18:25.000Z
title: Pro-Iran Explosive Media trolls Trump with AI-generated Lego cartoons
author: David Gilbert, wired.com
summary_long: Minutes after President Donald Trump announced that he would not wipe out “a whole civilization” on Tuesday evening, a team of self-described young Iranian activists jumped into action. Members of the group known as Explosive Media were putting the finishing touches on their latest AI-generated, Lego-inspired Trump video. The video features a Trump mini-figure colluding with leaders from Gulf states, Iranian officials pressing a big red button labeled “back to the stone age,” and Trump throwing a chair at US generals. This was the latest of more than a dozen videos the pro-Iran group has released since the beginning of the war in February, many of which have racked up millions of views on mainstream platforms. Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-04-10. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: bigtech:dad stuck in support nightmare after teen lied about age on :2026-04-10T11:30:45.000Z
title: Dad stuck in support nightmare after teen lied about age on Discord
author: Ashley Belanger
summary_long: Brady Frey did not realize that his daughter lied about her age when she set up her Discord account. He only found out after her account got hacked and he got trapped in a spiraling support nightmare while trying to stop the hacker from targeting dozens of her young friends with financial extortion scams. When Frey's daughter signed up for Discord, she was 12 and technically not old enough to have an account. But like many kids who, regulators have found, commonly lie about their age to access social media platforms, she didn't want to wait another year to join her friends on the messaging app. Source: arstechnica.com. Date: 2026-04-10. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: policy:friday squid blogging squid overfishing in the south pacific:2026-04-10T21:03:27.000Z
title: Friday Squid Blogging: Squid Overfishing in the South Pacific
author: Bruce Schneier
summary_long: Regulation is hard: > The South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organization (SPRFMO) oversees fishing across roughly 59 million square kilometers (22 million square miles) of the South Pacific high seas, trying to impose order on a region double the size of Africa, where distant-water fleets pursue species ranging from jack mackerel to jumbo flying squid. The latter dominated this year’s talks. > > Fishing for jumbo flying squid (Dosidicus gigas) has expanded rapidly over the past two decades. The number of squid-jigging vessels operating in SPRFMO waters rose from 14 in 2000 to more than 500 last year, almost all of them flying the Chinese flag. Source: www.schneier.com. Date: 2026-04-10. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: consumer:estonia is the rare eu country opposing child social media b:2026-04-10T19:46:20.000Z
title: Estonia is the rare EU country opposing child social media bans
author: Will Shanklin
summary_long: As child social media bans spread across Europe and beyond, Estonia isn't having it. On Friday, the country's education minister said the bans won't "actually solve problems," while warning that the kids will find a way regardless. Although companies like Meta would love for you to believe it’s a fairy tale, social media addiction is associated with tangible negative repercussions for children. Studies show that its harms range from depression and anxiety to sleep deprivation and obesity. Source: www.engadget.com. Date: 2026-04-10. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: consumer:french government says au revoir windows bienvenue linux:2026-04-10T16:54:07.000Z
title: French government says au revoir Windows, bienvenue Linux
author: Will Shanklin
summary_long: America's Big Tech companies may soon learn that saddling up with Donald Trump doesn't tend to work out in the end. As the president sows chaos and distrust around the globe while taking aim at EU tech regulations, Europe is looking for ways to adopt its own alternatives. The latest example is France, which said it's dropping Microsoft Windows in favor of Linux. On Wednesday, France said (via TechCrunch) it plans to move its workstations from Windows to the open-source Linux. Source: www.engadget.com. Date: 2026-04-10. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: consumer:the artemis ii astronauts are back after a 10day journey aro:2026-04-11T03:38:00.000Z
title: The Artemis II astronauts are back after a 10-day journey around the moon
author: Mariella Moon
summary_long: The Orion capsule carrying the Artemis II astronauts has successfully splashed down off the coast of San Diego at 8:07PM Eastern time on April 10. It signals the conclusion of Artemis II’s 10-day journey around the moon, which is meant to be a test flight for a future mission that would bring humanity back to the lunar surface. The Orion crew module carrying the mission’s astronauts separated from the service module at 7:33 PM. While the service module was designed to burn up in the Earth’s atmosphere, the crew capsule was built to bring the astronauts back home safely. Source: www.engadget.com. Date: 2026-04-11. Why it matters: outline likely impact on technology, business, or society. What’s next: note near-term developments or signals to watch.

id: space:nasa confident artemis 2 heat shield will protect crew durin:2026-04-10T17:42:33.000Z
title: NASA confident Artemis 2 heat shield will protect crew during re-entry
author: William Harwood
summary_long: When the Artemis 2 Orion crew capsule returns to Earth after flying around the moon, it will hit the discernible atmosphere some 75 miles above the Pacific Ocean at a blistering 24,000 mph, fast enough to fly from New York to London in less than 10 minutes. Within seconds, temperatures across its 16.5-foot-wide heat shield will climb to some 5,000 degrees — half as hot as the visible surface of the sun — as the ship rapidly slows in an electrically charged fireball of atmospheric friction. The four astronauts on board — Artemis 2 commander Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch and Canadian astronaut Jeremy Hansen — are counting on the heat shield to keep them safe, in a comfortable environment, all the way through the peak heating zone before a parachute-assisted splashdown in the Pacific. “We have high confidence in the system, in the heat shield and the parachutes and the recovery systems we put together,” Amit Kshatriya, NASA’s…
